findings.description: Classic N+1: low_stock_report fires a separate movements COUNT(*) query per low-stock product (N+1 round-trips); collapse into a single GROUP BY aggregate LEFT JOINed against the products query so the whole report is one statement.

search_by_name uses a leading-wildcard LIKE '%fragment%', which is non-sargable and forces a full table scan of products on every admin search (no index can help); consider FTS5 / a trigram index or at minimum a prefix LIKE 'fragment%'.

Every public method opens and closes a brand-new SQLite connection via _connect(); when these methods are driven in a loop by the pipeline this repeats connection setup / schema parsing / page-cache discard each call, so reuse a single long-lived connection or a pool.

Hot predicates rely on indexes that are unverifiable in scope: products.sku (get_product/reserve/restock) and a composite movements(sku, created_at) for the 30-day count; absent these, every lookup and the per-product COUNT degrade to table scans.

search_by_name (and the initial products fetch at line 76) run unbounded SELECTs with no LIMIT and materialize every matching row into a Product list; a broad fragment on a large catalog loads the entire result set into memory at once.
Deferred to critic-security: fragment is string-interpolated directly into the SQL (f-string), a textbook SQL injection vector; should be a parameterized LIKE with ? binding.

Deferred to critic-logic: reserve uses 'row["stock"] > quantity' so a request for exactly the available stock is rejected (off-by-one; should be >=), and the read-then-UPDATE is a non-atomic check that races under concurrency.
